questions.question:
Consider the language L = {a^n | n >= 0} union {a^n b^m | n != m} and the following statements.
I. L is deterministic context-free.
II. L is context-free but not deterministic context-free.
III. L is not LL(k) for any k.
Which of the above statements is/are TRUE?
A. I only
B. II only
C. I and III only
D. III only
questions.to_find: Which statements about the language L = {a^n | n >= 0} union {a^n b^m | n != m} are TRUE.

understand.plain: We have a language L that consists of all strings of only a's, combined with strings a^n b^m where the number of a's is not equal to the number of b's. We need to classify this language (DCFL, CFL, LL(k)) and check which statements hold.

keywords.explain: A language accepted by a deterministic pushdown automaton (DPDA), which makes exactly one choice at each step.
keywords.example: {a^n b^n | n >= 0} is DCFL because a DPDA can push a's and pop for each b.

keywords.explain: A class of context-free grammars parseable top-down with k tokens of lookahead. LL(k) grammars are a strict subset of DCFLs.
keywords.example: Most simple programming language grammars are LL(1) or LL(k).

keywords.explain: L1 union L2 contains all strings that are in L1 or L2 (or both).
keywords.example: {a*} union {a^n b^m | n != m} includes 'aaa', 'ab', 'aabb' but not 'ab'... wait, 'ab' has n=1, m=1 so n=m, excluded from second set but 'a' is in first set.

understand.visual_alt: Language L combines two parts: pure a-strings and a^n b^m strings where counts differ.

given.aim: Determine the classification of L = {a^n | n >= 0} union {a^n b^m | n != m}.

terms.meaning: All strings consisting only of a's (including empty string), which is a regular language.
terms.connects: Being regular (and hence DCFL), its union with L2 determines L's class.

terms.meaning: Strings of a's followed by b's where counts are unequal.
terms.example: ab (1 a, 2 b? no, ab has 1a and 1b so equal, excluded), 'aab' (2a,1b, included), 'abb' (1a,2b, included).
terms.connects: L2 = (a^+ b^* union a^* b^+) minus {a^n b^n} which is context-free.

terms.meaning: The complement of the canonical CFL {a^n b^n | n >= 0} is also a CFL (and in fact a DCFL).
terms.example: Strings over {a,b}* that are NOT of the form a^n b^n, i.e., interleaved, or unequal counts.
terms.connects: L2 intersected with a^* b^* gives exactly those strings with unequal counts, which is DCFL.

given.plan: Recognize that L = complement of {a^n b^n | n >= 1} over {a,b}*. Since {a^n b^n} is a DCFL, its complement is also DCFL. Then check if L is LL(k) for any k by examining whether it needs unbounded lookahead.

steps.title: Identify the complement relationship
steps.apply: The complement of {a^n b^n | n >= 1} over {a,b}* consists of all strings over {a,b}* that are NOT of the form a^n b^n (n >= 1). This includes: epsilon, all pure a-strings, all pure b-strings, all a^n b^m with n != m, and all strings with b before a. Now L = {a^n | n >= 0} union {a^n b^m | n != m}. Observe that L contains all strings in a^* b^* except {a^n b^n | n >= 1}, which is exactly complement({a^n b^n | n >= 1}) restricted to a^* b^* plus epsilon. Actually, L = {a^n | n >= 0} union {a^n b^m | m >= 1, n != m} = a^* union {a^n b^m | n >= 0, m >= 1, n != m}. This equals all strings in a^* b^* except {a^n b^n | n >= 1}. Since {a^n b^n | n >= 1} is a DCFL, its complement within a^* b^* is also DCFL. Statement I is TRUE.
steps.note: DCFLs are closed under complement, so complement of a DCFL is DCFL.

steps.title: Check LL(k) property
steps.formula_raw: LL(k) grammars form a strict subset of DCFLs
steps.apply: For L to be LL(k), any grammar for L must be parseable with k tokens lookahead. Consider a string starting with a^n. A parser cannot determine with finite k lookahead whether it will see 'n b's' (which would be a^n b^n, NOT in L) or '!= n b's' (which IS in L), until it has read all b's. No finite k suffices to distinguish these cases in general. Therefore L is not LL(k) for any fixed k. Statement III is TRUE.
steps.note: LL(k) requires bounded lookahead; here the distinction requires reading arbitrary-length b-strings.

steps.title: Check Statement II
steps.apply: Statement II says L is CFL but NOT DCFL. But we showed L IS a DCFL (deterministic context-free). So Statement II is FALSE.
steps.note: Statements I and III are true, II is false — answer is C.

solution.result: Statements I and III are TRUE. Answer: C. I and III only.

questions.formula_note: DCFLs are closed under complement; LL(k) is a strict subset of DCFL requiring bounded lookahead, which this language does not admit.

questions.question:
Consider the following languages:
L_1 = {uvuv | u, v in (0+1)*}
L_2 = {xy | x, y in (0+1)*, |x| = |y|, x != y}
Which one of the following is TRUE?
A. L_1 is regular and L_2 is context-free.
B. L_1 is context-free but not regular and L_2 is context-free.
C. Neither L_1 nor L_2 is context-free.
D. L_1 is context-free but L_2 is not context-free.
questions.answer: C. Neither L_1 nor L_2 is context-free.
questions.to_find: Classify L_1 = {uvuv | u,v in (0+1)*} and L_2 = {xy | x,y in (0+1)*, |x|=|y|, x != y} in the Chomsky hierarchy.

understand.plain: L_1 contains strings of even length that can be split into two equal halves that are identical (like concatenating a string with itself). L_2 contains strings of even length where the two halves are different. We need to determine if each is regular, context-free, or neither.

keywords.explain: The language {ww | w in Sigma*} (a string concatenated with itself) is a classic example of a context-sensitive language that is NOT context-free.
keywords.example: 0110|0110 is in {ww}, but no PDA can check w=w for arbitrary w.

keywords.explain: The language of strings of even length where the two halves differ. Its classification follows from closure properties.
keywords.example: 01|10 (halves differ) is in complement of WW restricted to even-length strings.

keywords.explain: A property that all CFLs must satisfy: any sufficiently long string can be 'pumped' (repeated portions). If a language fails this, it is not context-free.
keywords.example: Applying pumping lemma to {ww} shows it is not CFL.

understand.visual_alt: L_1 is the WW language over binary alphabet; L_2 is the complement within even-length strings.

given.aim: Classify L_1 (WW language) and L_2 (complement of WW among equal-length pairs) in the Chomsky hierarchy.

terms.meaning: This is exactly the WW language over {0,1}: all strings of the form ww where w = uv is any binary string.
terms.connects: WW is known to be context-sensitive but not context-free.

terms.meaning: All binary strings of even length where the first half differs from the second half.
terms.example: 0110 has x=01, y=10, x != y, so 0110 is in L_2. 0101 has x=01, y=01, x=y so NOT in L_2.
terms.connects: L_2 is the complement of L_1 within even-length strings over (0+1)*.

terms.meaning: CFLs are NOT closed under complement or intersection with another CFL.
terms.example: If L_1 were CFL, its complement would not necessarily be CFL.
terms.connects: Since L_1 is not CFL, we need to separately analyze L_2.

given.plan: Use the pumping lemma to show L_1 is not CFL. Then observe that L_2 is the complement of L_1 (within even-length strings), and show L_2 is also not CFL using a similar argument or reduction.

steps.title: Show L_1 is not context-free
steps.apply: L_1 = {ww | w in (0+1)*} is the classic WW language. By the pumping lemma for CFLs: suppose L_1 is CFL with pumping length p. Consider the string s = 0^p 1^p 0^p 1^p (here w = 0^p 1^p). For any split s = uvxyz with |vxy| <= p and |vy| >= 1, the substring vxy lies within one of the four blocks or spans at most two adjacent blocks. Pumping (increasing i in uv^i x y^i z) disrupts the equal-halves structure. The string cannot remain in L_1. Therefore L_1 is NOT context-free.
steps.note: WW is context-sensitive (accepted by a linear-bounded automaton) but not context-free.

steps.title: Show L_2 is not context-free
steps.formula_raw: CFL intersection with regular = CFL; CFL closed under union
steps.apply: Suppose L_2 were CFL. Consider L_2 intersected with 0^* 1^* 0^* 1^* (which is regular). The intersection is {0^a 1^b 0^c 1^d | a+b = c+d, (a != c or b != d)}. Now L_1 intersected with 0^* 1^* 0^* 1^* = {0^n 1^m 0^n 1^m} which is not CFL. Since L_1' = (L_2 intersect 0^*1^*0^*1^*) union {0^n 1^m 0^n 1^m} = all of 0^*1^*0^*1^* with even-length structure... More directly: if L_2 is CFL, then (complement of L_2) union {odd-length strings} = L_1 union {odd-length strings}. Since odd-length strings is regular and union of CFL with regular is CFL, L_1 would be CFL — contradiction. Therefore L_2 is NOT context-free.
steps.note: If complement of L_1 (within even-length) were CFL, then by closure properties L_1 would be CFL, which it is not.

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.apply: L_1 is not CFL and L_2 is not CFL. So neither L_1 nor L_2 is context-free. This matches option C.
steps.note: Both languages are context-sensitive (Type 1) but not Type 2.

solution.result: Neither L_1 nor L_2 is context-free. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Pumping lemma for CFLs applied to WW language; closure properties of CFLs (closed under union with regular, intersection with regular) used to show L_2 is also not CFL.

questions.question:
Let L_R be a regular language and L_2 be a context-free language. Which of the following languages is/are context-free?
A. L_R / L_2 (right quotient of L_R by L_2)
B. complement(L_R) union L_2
C. L_R intersection L_2
D. L_R - L_2 (set difference)
questions.to_find: Identify which of the given language operations on a regular language L_R and a CFL L_2 yield a context-free language.

understand.plain: We are given a regular language L_R and a context-free language L_2, and asked which combinations (quotient, union with complement, intersection, set difference) are guaranteed to be context-free. We apply closure properties of CFLs.

keywords.explain: A language class is 'closed' under an operation if applying that operation to languages in the class always gives a language in the same class.
keywords.example: CFLs are closed under union: if L1 and L2 are CFLs, L1 union L2 is also CFL.

keywords.explain: L1 / L2 = {x | exists y in L2 such that xy in L1}. It removes suffixes from L1 that belong to L2.

keywords.term: CFL closure under intersection with regular
keywords.explain: The intersection of a CFL with a regular language is always a CFL.
keywords.example: {a^n b^n | n >= 0} intersection a^* b^* = {a^n b^n | n >= 0}, still CFL.

understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing closure/non-closure of CFLs under various operations: union with regular (closed), intersection with regular (closed), complement (not closed), difference (not closed).

given.aim: Determine which operations on L_R (regular) and L_2 (CFL) produce a CFL.

terms.meaning: Accepted by a finite automaton; closed under all boolean operations.
terms.connects: Regular languages are a subset of CFLs; CFLs are closed under intersection with regular.

terms.meaning: Accepted by a pushdown automaton; closed under union, concatenation, Kleene star, intersection with regular, but NOT closed under intersection or complement.
terms.connects: Key tool: CFL intersect Regular = CFL; Regular union CFL = CFL.

terms.meaning: L1 - L2 = L1 intersection complement(L2). CFLs are not closed under complement, so difference of two CFLs may not be CFL.
terms.example: {a^n b^n c^n | n >= 0} can be expressed as difference of two CFLs — and it is NOT CFL.
terms.connects: L_R - L_2 = L_R intersection complement(L_2). complement(L_2) need not be CFL, so the result is uncertain.

given.plan: Apply known closure properties: CFLs closed under union with regular, intersection with regular. CFLs NOT closed under complement. Analyze each option.

steps.title: Option A: L_R / L_2 (right quotient of regular by CFL)
steps.formula_raw: L_R / L_2 = {x | exists y in L_2, xy in L_R}
steps.apply: The right quotient of a regular language by ANY language is always regular. This is because we can construct a DFA for L_R and for each state q, check if from q there is a path via some string in L_2 to a final state. Since L_R is regular, L_R / L_2 is regular, hence also context-free. Wait — but is this correct for arbitrary L_2? Yes: L_R / L_2 is always regular when L_R is regular (regardless of L_2's class). So A is context-free (in fact regular). However, looking at the image again for the exact options shown...
steps.note: Quotient of regular language by any language is regular, hence CFL.

steps.title: Option B: complement(L_R) union L_2
steps.formula_raw: complement of regular is regular; CFL union regular = CFL
steps.apply: complement(L_R) is regular (regulars closed under complement). Regular union CFL = CFL (CFLs closed under union with regular, since regular is a subset of CFL and CFLs are closed under union). Therefore complement(L_R) union L_2 is CFL. Option B IS context-free.
steps.note: This is a standard closure result.

steps.title: Option C: L_R intersection L_2
steps.apply: The intersection of a CFL with a regular language is always CFL (proven by running PDA for L_2 and DFA for L_R in parallel — product construction). Therefore L_R intersection L_2 is CFL. Option C IS context-free.
steps.note: This is one of the most important closure results for CFLs.

steps.title: Option D: L_R - L_2 (set difference)
steps.apply: complement(L_2) need not be CFL (CFLs not closed under complement). L_R intersection complement(L_2): even though L_R is regular, this is regular intersect (complement of CFL). The complement of a CFL is not necessarily CFL, and intersection of regular with non-CFL need not be CFL. Counterexample: Let L_R = (a+b+c)* and L_2 = {a^n b^n | n >= 0} union {a^n b^m c^n | n,m >= 0}... Actually, a simpler argument: L_2 may have a non-CFL complement. L_R - L_2 = L_R intersect complement(L_2) is not guaranteed to be CFL. Option D is NOT necessarily context-free.
steps.note: Set difference of regular minus CFL is not guaranteed to be CFL.

solution.result: Options B and C are context-free. Answer: B, C.

questions.formula_note: Key closure properties: CFL closed under union with regular and intersection with regular; CFL NOT closed under complement or set difference with CFL.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?
A. Every subset of a recursively enumerable language is recursive.
B. If a language L and its complement are both recursively enumerable, then L must be recursive.
C. Complement of a context-free language must be recursive.
D. If L_1 and L_2 are regular, then L_1 ∩ L_2 must be deterministic context-free.
questions.to_find: Identify which statements about language classes (recursive, RE, CFL, regular) are TRUE.

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of relationships between language classes: recursive (decidable), recursively enumerable (RE/semi-decidable), context-free, and regular. We need to verify each statement using known closure properties and theorems.

keywords.explain: A language accepted by a Turing machine (TM may loop on some inputs). Not necessarily decidable.
keywords.example: The halting problem language is RE but not recursive.

keywords.explain: A language for which there exists a TM that always halts (accepts or rejects). Also called 'decidable'.
keywords.example: Any CFL is recursive (CFLs are decidable).

keywords.explain: If L is RE and complement(L) is also RE, then L is recursive (decidable). This follows from running both TMs in parallel.
keywords.example: If we can semi-decide both L and its complement, we can decide L.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 480 300' width='480' height='300'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><ellipse cx='240' cy='150' rx='220' ry='130' fill='#f8f0e0' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='240' cy='160' rx='170' ry='100' fill='#e8f0f8' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='240' cy='170' rx='120' ry='70' fill='#e8f8e8' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='240' cy='180' rx='70' ry='42' fill='#f0e8f8' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='240' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Regular</text><text x='240' y='240' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>CFL</text><text x='240' y='265' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>Recursive</text><text x='240' y='286' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>RE</text><text x='30' y='40' class='sub mut'>RE (Type 0)</text><text x='30' y='58' class='sub mut'>Recursive (decidable)</text><text x='30' y='76' class='sub ink'>Context-Free (Type 2)</text><text x='30' y='94' class='sub ink'>Regular (Type 3)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Chomsky hierarchy diagram showing Regular subset of CFL subset of Recursive subset of RE.

given.aim: Evaluate four statements about recursive, RE, CFL, and regular language classes.

terms.meaning: A subset of an RE language need not be RE (let alone recursive). RE is not closed under subset.
terms.example: The halting problem H is RE. The set of Turing machines that halt in even steps is a subset of H but may have any complexity.
terms.connects: Statement A claims every subset of RE is recursive — this is FALSE.

terms.term: L and complement(L) both RE => L recursive
terms.meaning: This is a theorem: if both L and its complement are RE, then L is decidable (recursive). Proof: run TM for L and TM for complement(L) in parallel; one must halt.
terms.example: Halting problem is RE but its complement is not RE; so halting problem is not recursive.

terms.meaning: Every CFL is decidable; CFG membership can be decided by CYK algorithm in O(n^3). Therefore complement of CFL is also recursive.
terms.example: {a^n b^n | n >= 0} is CFL hence recursive; its complement is also recursive.
terms.connects: Statement C is TRUE — complement of CFL is recursive.

given.plan: Evaluate each statement using theorems: (A) RE not closed under subset — FALSE; (B) L and co-L both RE implies L recursive — TRUE; (C) CFL is subset of recursive, so complement of CFL is recursive — TRUE; (D) Regular closed under intersection, result is regular which is DCFL — TRUE.

steps.title: Statement A: Every subset of a recursively enumerable language is recursive
steps.apply: FALSE. Consider: the halting problem H is RE. The subset K = {<M> | M is a TM that accepts its own encoding} is also RE but not recursive. Moreover, any RE language is a subset of the universal language Sigma* which is regular (hence recursive). If statement A were true, every language would be recursive — absurd. Statement A is FALSE.
steps.note: RE is not closed downward; subsets of RE can be anywhere in the hierarchy.

steps.title: Statement B: L and complement(L) both RE => L is recursive
steps.formula_raw: Run TM_L and TM_{complement(L)} in parallel
steps.apply: TRUE. This is a standard theorem. If TM1 accepts L and TM2 accepts complement(L), then for any input x: run TM1 and TM2 in parallel (dovetailing). Since every string is either in L or complement(L), one of them will eventually accept. So we get a TM that always halts — L is recursive. Statement B is TRUE.
steps.note: This is equivalent to: L is recursive iff both L and complement(L) are RE.

steps.title: Statement C: Complement of a CFL is recursive
steps.formula_raw: CFL subset of Recursive; Recursive closed under complement
steps.apply: TRUE. Every CFL is recursive (CYK algorithm decides any CFL in polynomial time). The class of recursive languages is closed under complement. Therefore, if L is CFL, L is recursive, and complement(L) is also recursive. Statement C is TRUE.
steps.note: Note: complement of CFL need not be CFL (CFLs not closed under complement), but it IS recursive.

steps.title: Statement D: If L_1 and L_2 are regular, then L_1 intersection L_2 is deterministic context-free
steps.formula_raw: Regular subset of DCFL; DCFL closed under intersection with regular
steps.apply: TRUE. If L_1 and L_2 are regular, then L_1 intersection L_2 is also regular (regular languages closed under intersection). Since every regular language is a DCFL, L_1 intersection L_2 is deterministic context-free. Statement D is TRUE.
steps.note: Regular is a strict subset of DCFL, so the intersection of two regular languages is trivially DCFL.

solution.result: Statements B, C, and D are TRUE. Answer: B, C, D.

questions.formula_note: Key theorems: L recursive iff L and complement(L) are both RE; CFL is decidable (CYK); regular languages form a strict subset of DCFL; recursive languages are closed under complement.
